blocks.content: I moved into my new room 3 days ago.
Since then, I found myself staring at the window quite a few times. It’s the only window in my room, and I can look through it no matter where I am standing in the room.
I didn’t like the window at first.

It was actually the reason I could not move in earlier. I spent a couple of days living around stacks of boxes in my old dorm room, waiting for this new window to be finished. Every time I asked, the response was to wait a little longer.
For the window.
The first time I looked at the window, I judged it.
I looked at the frame around it, reminding myself that there would be painters coming next Saturday to paint it. I stared at the two stickers on the window. Glanced at the view.
Unremarkable.
Especially since there was a piece of metal blocking most of the view.
The second time I looked out the window, it felt ominous.
Everything was pitch black, except for the glow of a single lamp from the neighbouring house.

Very faintly, I could make out a door beside the lamp. Its hollow frame outlining a window of its own. The door’s window was completely dark. The glow of the lamp stopped at the edge where the window started, the weak remaining bit of light unable to penetrate through the many layers of darkness behind the window.
I wondered if someone was also staring out of that window. I couldn’t see them, but could they see me? The light in my room was on, so they probably could.

My mind began imagining silhouettes behind the window. With the glow of the lamp faintly grazing the obsidian-like glass, the silhouettes began floating closer towards the line between imagination and reality.
A little too close to the line.
I looked away.
I don’t exactly recall the next few times I looked through the window.
I looked through it in passing.

Quick pauses between the daily tasks I busied myself with. Studying for exams. Afternoon naps. And of course, very slow efforts of gradually breaking up the clusters of boxes and suitcases as items began to find places to live in my room. A plant I was helping a friend take care of would end up sitting on the windowsill.
What I do remember though, were the last few times I looked out the window.
One of those times was this morning.
This time, when I looked out the window, it was against my will.

I was sitting at my desk, gazing intensely at my execution stack in silence. As I traced through my Binary Search algorithm to find the middle index at that point, I was startled by the whir of a drill, followed closely by an intense succession of loud cracks.
Although I didn’t want to disrupt my gaze, the sound reflexively made me peel my eyes away to look out the window.

It was then that I got startled the second time, when I saw a man forcefully using a drill to push something into the outside frame of the window. Perhaps the most unprepared I had ever been in an interaction, I stared at the man for a few seconds. He looked at me. I looked at him. And then I stood up, took my things, and left the room.
The next time I looked out the window, the man was gone.

I had finished making my execution stack for the Binary Search algorithm and was trying to think of a topic for today’s Substack post. As I was thinking, I naturally began looking out the window. My mind began to wander, and it landed on a thought: this angle looking outside the window would be a really cool shot. Especially paired with a shot from the same angle at night with the glowing lamp and possible silhouettes.
So I took out my camera. Lined up the shot. And took the picture.
It was then that I decided it was the perfect time to take an afternoon nap.
The last time I looked out the window, I was trying to take my afternoon nap.
Almost immediately as I laid down, I began thinking about the picture I just took. Looking out the window from my bed, I realized I had unlocked a new view.

From where I was, I could see a bit more of the brick wall that the neighbouring home had. Dangling over the straight, rigid lines of grey concrete surrounding the yellow-orange brick, there was a tangle of branches. Twisting and knotting together, they created a mesh of green leaves that fluttered in the soft breeze.
Admiring the leaves, I wondered what other views I could discover in the months and maybe even years to come.
What could I see if I stood near the lower left corner of my room?
How would the summer sun look shining through the glass in the morning?
What about the first winter snowflakes floating down from the evening sky?
What thoughts would be passing through my mind as I looked out?
Would they be fleeting glances between tasks during those weeks that seemingly flew by?
Or would they be long stares, deep in thought, during those hours that seemingly never end?

The window never changed. It was still the same window that had most of its view covered by a piece of metal. Deemed unremarkable when I first moved in. Yet, looking at it now, I cannot imagine feeling the same way about it as I did the first time.
